title: Computing Standard Curve Quality Control Metrics using curveRmetrics
text: vignette demonstrates end--end curveRmetrics workflow evaluating standarded curve quality. Starting raw Frequentist Bayesian curve fits stored database, derive standardized set Quality Control (QC) metrics: Inflection point — point standard curve concavity transitions concave concave . point assay sensitive measurement errors measured response assay. Assay sensitivity — Measured slope inflection point. Limits Detection (LODs) — Lower upper LODs defined upper 97.5% confidence bound lower asymptote lower 2.5% confidence bound upper asymptote, respectively (Rajam et al.). Reliable Detection Limits (RDLs) - Lower RDL: lowest concentration assay consistently produces signal background 95% confidence based fit standard curve (Rajam et al.). Upper RDL: Analogously, highest concentration assay consistently produces signal upper asymptote (saturation) 95% confidence, based fit standard curve. Minimum Detectable Concentration - smallest antibody concentration produces signal assay can detect background (Rajam et al.). corresponds x-coordinate Lower Limit Detection legend, concentration axis. Limits Quantification (LOQ) - Defines region assay response (MFI) concentration sample estimates less measurement error. Limits Quantification derived local minimum maximum second derivative x given y standard curve (Daly et al.), (Jeanne L Sebaugh P. D. McCray) , (Sanz et al.). metrics computed natural (back-transformed) parameter units results directly interpretable comparable across fitting methods.

what: Setup: Load Curve Data from Database
title: Computing Standard Curve Quality Control Metrics using curveRmetrics
text: load Frequentist Bayesian curve fits curveTest2 study containing data ELISA immunoassays, along parameter confidence/credible intervals assay standards. studies similar fit data standard curves can used input long follow functional forms accompanying curveRfreq stanassay R packages. Curve fits multiplex bead arrays immunoassays standard curve can used. Curve fit results Frequentist Bayesian fitting methods combined single data frame downstream QC functions operate Frequentist Bayesian curves simultaneously, enabling direct method comparisons. Note: transform_to_natural_units() back-transforms four curve parameters (, b, c, d) fitted scales natural units. Select specific curve_id visualize comparisons. curveRmetrics package compute Quality Control (QC) metrics curve_id naturalized parameter set (curves_nat). verbose argument curveRmetrics shows QC output messages. hide messages set verbose FALSE.

text: method-specific parameter list built curve: Frequentist Grid parameters use \(b_{nat} = b_{raw} \cdot \ln(10)\) \(c_{nat} = 10^{c_{raw}}\).  eval_fn closure computes \(d^2(\log_{10} y)/d(\log_{10} x)^2\) via log10(10^.evaluate_curve(p_raw, log10(x))). Bayesian Grid parameters use \(b_{nat} = 1/b_{raw}\) raw natural EC50 \(c_{raw}\).  eval_fn closure computes \(d^2(\log_{10} y)/d(\log_{10} x)^2\) via log10(.evaluate_curve(p_nat, log(x))). local() used constructing eval_fn force eager capture p_raw / p_nat, preventing R's lazy-evaluation scoping causing closures share (final-iteration) environment.

text: Computes \(d^2y/du^2\) \(u = \log_{10}(x)\) using non-uniform central-difference formula stable log-spaced grids. Working \(\log_{10}(x)\) space avoids \(1/x^2\) blow-occur differentiating natural concentration.

title: Non-uniform central-difference second derivative in log10-x space — .d2y_nonuniform
text: formula interior point \(\) : $$   \frac{d^2y}{du^2}\bigg|_i \approx   \frac{2}{h_l + h_r}   \left[\frac{y_{+1} - y_i}{h_r} - \frac{y_i - y_{-1}}{h_l}\right] $$ \(h_l = u_i - u_{-1}\) \(h_r = u_{+1} - u_i\).

title: Compute analytical inflection x-coordinate from normalised parameters — .inflection_x
text: logistic4: y = d + (-d) / (1 + exp((x-c)/b)) Symmetric => inflection exactly x = c, y = (+d)/2 logistic5: y = d + (-d) / (1 + exp((x-c)/b))^g Set d²y/dx² = 0: let z = exp((x-c)/b) Numerator d²y/dx² proportional : (g+1)z - (b-1)/(b... ) Full derivation => z* = (b-1) / (bg + 1)  requires b > 1 z > 0 => x_infl = c + b * log(z*) loglogistic4: y = + (d-) / (1 + (x/c)^b) Symmetric log(x) scale => inflection x = c loglogistic5: y = + (d-) * (1 + gexp(-b(x-c)))^(-1/g) Set d²y/dx² = 0: let u = gexp(-b(x-c)) => u* = (b-1)/(b+1)  ... simplified Richards condition  requires b > 1 => x_infl = c - (log(u*) - log(g)) / b = c + (log(g) - log((b-1)/(b+1))) / b gompertz4: y = + (d-) * exp(-exp(-b*(x-c))) Set d²y/dx² = 0: let u = exp(-b*(x-c)) => u*(1 - u) = 1/b ... simplifies u* = (b-1)/b  (requires b > 1) => x_infl = c - log(u*) / b = c + log(b/(b-1)) / b inflection: y_infl = + (d-)*exp(-1)  (always ~36.8% range ) Derivations use substitution \(t = \ln(x/c)\): logistic4 Symmetric \(\Rightarrow x^* = c\). logistic5 \(z^* = (b-1)/(bg+1)\), \(x^* = c \cdot z^{*1/b}\). loglogistic4 Symmetric \(\Rightarrow x^* = c\). loglogistic5 \(u^* = (b-1)/(b+1)\), \(x^* = c \exp((\ln g - \ln u^*)/b)\). gompertz4 \(u^* = (b-1)/b\), \(x^* = c \exp(-\ln u^*/b)\).

text: Derivations use \(t = \ln(x/c)\), \(u = \log_{10}(x)\): logistic4 \(z^2 - 4z + 1 = 0\), \(z^* = 2 \pm \sqrt{3}\), \(u^* = u_c + b \cdot \log_{10}(2 \pm \sqrt{3})\) (exact). logistic5 logistic4 seeds scaled \(g^{0.3}\) (approx). loglogistic4 \(u^* = u_c \pm \log_{10}(2 \pm \sqrt{3}) / b\) (exact). loglogistic5 loglogistic4 seeds scaled \(g^{0.3}\) (approx). gompertz4 \(v^* = (3 \pm \sqrt{5})/2\), \(u^* = u_c \pm \mathrm{LN\_PHI2} / (b \cdot \ln 10)\) (exact).
